title: Field Sampling and Laboratory Analysis of Microzooplankton in Small Wetlands with Sanitary-Biological Assessment
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"p9rc7","text":"This protocol describes standardized field collection and laboratory processing of microzooplankton from small stagnant wetlands (peaty, humic-rich waters). It includes multi-microbiotope sampling, sample settling, microscopic examination (100–400×), taxonomic identification, and qualitative sanitary-biological (saprobic) assessment.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"kkrgm","text":"**Field Equipment:**","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fu8kr","text":"- 0.5–1 L plastic containers (3–5)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"h6zc9","text":"- GPS device","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"n1e1a","text":"- Labels and field notebook","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"g1a1p","text":"- Camera","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vxkmm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tijmc","text":"**Laboratory Equipment:**","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jwvpc","text":"- Stereomicroscope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e9l8h","text":"- Compound microscope (100–400×)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1sbyb","text":"- Glass slides \u000026 coverslips","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lymjs","text":"- Pasteur pipettes \u000026 Petri dishes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"osbvo","text":"- Taxonomic identification keys","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Record exact GPS coordinates 50.991293, 34.473122. Describe water depth (0.3–0.8 m), vegetation (sedges, sphagnum, duckweed), water colour, peat layer, and visible disturbances. Document the site and each micro-biotope via photography.

Collect 3–5 samples (0.5–1 L each) from different micro-biotypes (open water, among duckweed/sedges, near sphagnum, bottom detritus). Gently submerge containers to collect water with surface detritus, plant fragments, and sediment. Label immediately and transport in shade; process within 2–4 hours.
Allow samples to settle at room temperature for 30–90 minutes. It is critical not to stir or agitate the containers after the settling period.
Using a pipette, take subsamples from the surface, middle, and bottom layers. Place 1–2 drops on a slide and apply a coverslip. Prepare 5–10 slides per sample to ensure representative coverage.

Conduct initial screening under a stereomicroscope (10–40×) for larger organisms (Daphnia, insect larvae). Transition to a compound microscope at 100× for overview, and 200–400× for specific identification. Record taxa, relative abundance, and observed behavior.

Identify organisms using standard keys (e.g., Foissner for ciliates, Koste for rotifers). Focus specifically on Gastrotricha and free-living Nematoda_. Document findings with photos and video recordings.

Evaluate saprobity based on dominant groups. High numbers of saprophytic ciliates (_Paramecium, Vorticella, Stentor_), rotifers, nematodes, or gastrotrichs should be used to classify the saprobity level (e.g., β-mesosaprobic with α-elements).
Record all raw data and observations. If necessary, fix subsamples using 4% formaldehyde or Lugol’s solution. Archive all digital images and field notes.
creator: Evhenii Hordiienko
